fact: March 29 sits exactly 88 days into the year, which means there are exactly 277 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for earthworms to start their amazing job of eating dirt and creating super-rich soil that makes plants grow like crazy.
Cats often go bonkers around this time due to spring fever, and male cats start their loud midnight singing contests to impress potential mates.
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets almost exactly due west around March 29, making it one of the most balanced days of the year for directions.
Maple trees in cold regions are usually pumping out their sweetest sap right now, and you can actually hear the drip-drip-drip if you put your ear close to a maple tree with a tap in it.
March 29 often falls in the middle of 'mud season' in places with cold winters, when the ground becomes a squishy, boot-sucking mess that can literally steal your shoes.
Aries zodiac energy is at full blast on this date, which astrologers say makes the whole world feel more impatient and ready to start new adventures.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, March 29 typically marks the time when many spiders start spinning their biggest, most impressive webs before winter arrives.
The daylight on March 29 lasts almost exactly 12 hours in most places, but it's growing longer by about 3 minutes every single day in northern regions.
Frogs in ponds and marshes are usually at peak loudness around this date, with some species croaking so loudly they can be heard from over a mile away.
March 29 sits in the sweet spot where winter's last snowstorms can still surprise you, but spring thunderstorms are also starting to rumble in with their spectacular lightning shows.
This date often falls during the time when many flowers release their pollen in massive yellow clouds that coat everything in sight and make people sneeze like crazy.
